content.text: Anthropic Security and Compliance Due Diligence

Enterprise procurement of Anthropic's Claude API requires a structured security and compliance review before the contract can proceed. Unlike typical SaaS vendors, Anthropic offers an AI inference service with distinct risk considerations: the model processes potentially sensitive prompts, the service is cloud-hosted with no on-premise option, and data handling policies directly affect whether prompt content can be used to train future models. This document walks through each domain of the due diligence review, the artifacts you should request, and the questions your security and legal teams will need answered.

attrs.alt: Anthropic Security and Compliance Due Diligence Areas

content.text: Certifications and Attestations

Anthropic maintains several independent security certifications that anchor the due diligence review. These are third-party attestations — not self-assessments — and are the primary artifacts your information security team will use to evaluate the vendor.
Certification
Scope
Renewal Cadence
How to Obtain
SOC 2 Type II
Availability, confidentiality, and security of the Claude API and internal systems
Annual
Request directly from your Anthropic account executive under NDA
Information security management system (ISMS) covering API infrastructure
Annual surveillance + 3-year recert
Certificate publicly listed; full audit report under NDA
CSA STAR Level 1
Self-assessment against Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM)
As updated
Publicly available on CSA registry
HIPAA readiness
Covered entity and BAA availability for healthcare use cases
Aligned to SOC 2 scope
BAA available to qualifying enterprise customers; confirm current status with Anthropic

Always request the SOC 2 Type II report rather than the Type I. Type I attests that controls exist as of a point in time; Type II attests that controls operated effectively over a 6–12 month observation period. For production procurement, Type II is the minimum acceptable standard.
Reading the SOC 2 Report
The SOC 2 report will include an auditor opinion, a description of the system, and a list of control tests with results. Focus your review on:
Exceptions noted:
any control test marked as an exception requires follow-up. Ask Anthropic whether the exception has been remediated and whether it affects the services you will use.
Complementary user entity controls (CUECs):
these are controls the auditor expects your organization to implement. Review each CUEC and confirm your own team has those controls in place — the SOC 2 opinion is only valid when CUECs are met.
Observation period:
confirm the report covers at least 6 months and was issued within the last 12 months. Stale reports (>12 months old) should trigger a request for a bridge letter from Anthropic's auditor.
Data Handling and Retention

The most consequential due diligence question for most enterprises is: what happens to data sent to the Claude API? Anthropic's data handling policies have evolved as the product has matured. The following represents the current documented policy, but you must verify current terms with Anthropic directly before executing a contract.
Policy Area
Default Behavior
Enterprise Override Available?
Prompt and response logging
Anthropic logs API inputs and outputs for trust and safety monitoring and abuse detection
Zero-retention API mode available: no logs stored beyond the request lifecycle
Training data use
By default, API inputs/outputs may inform model evaluation; not used for training production models without explicit consent
API customers can opt out of all training data use; confirm current policy in DPA
Retention period
Logged data retained for up to 30 days by default for abuse detection
Zero-retention API bypasses retention entirely; some compliance tiers offer shorter windows
Data region
API infrastructure hosted on AWS; US regions by default
EU data residency available for qualifying tiers; verify current region availability
Zero-Retention API Mode

Enterprise customers processing regulated data (PII, PHI, financial data) typically require zero-retention mode. In this mode, Anthropic does not persist prompt content or API responses beyond the HTTP request lifecycle — no logs are written for the content of the call. Metadata (token counts, latency, error codes) may still be retained for billing and operational monitoring.

# Verify your API calls are landing in zero-retention mode
# (Anthropic account team confirms enablement at account level;
#  there is no per-call header to toggle this)

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# For compliance: confirm your org's API key is registered under a zero-retention agreement
# The API itself does not expose a response header confirming retention mode.
# Confirm with your account executive and document the confirmation in your risk register.

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8",
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise this contract clause: ..."}]
)

# Log metadata only (safe for any compliance tier); do NOT log response.content
# if the prompt contained PII or PHI
print(f"Tokens used: {response.usage.input_tokens + response.usage.output_tokens}")
print(f"Stop reason: {response.stop_reason}")

Zero-retention mode must be confirmed in writing by your Anthropic account executive and referenced in your Data Processing Agreement. A verbal assurance or a setting in the console is not sufficient for HIPAA or GDPR evidence packages.
Privacy Compliance and GDPR

If your organization processes data belonging to EU residents, or if your legal team requires GDPR compliance across all vendors, you will need to execute a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with Anthropic before sending any personal data to the API. The DPA governs the controller-processor relationship and specifies the legal basis for cross-border data transfer.
Data Processing Agreement (DPA):
request this from your account executive. It covers: the categories of personal data processed, the purposes and legal bases, the data subject rights procedures, and the sub-processor list.
Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs):
for EU-to-US transfers, Anthropic relies on SCCs (Module 2: Controller to Processor) as the transfer mechanism. Confirm that the SCCs are included as an annex to the DPA.
Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA):
some data protection authorities require a TIA for transfers to the US. Your DPO will need Anthropic's responses to the TIA questionnaire; request it from your account executive.
Data subject rights:
the DPA specifies how Anthropic supports your obligations for access, rectification, erasure, and portability requests. Understand the response SLA Anthropic commits to for DSR assistance.
Subprocessors and Supply Chain

Anthropic uses sub-processors — third parties that process data on Anthropic's behalf as part of delivering the API service. Under GDPR and most enterprise security policies, you must be aware of sub-processors and have a right to object to new additions.
Core Infrastructure

Anthropic's primary infrastructure sub-processor is Amazon Web Services (AWS). API requests are processed in AWS datacenters; the specific regions depend on your account configuration and data residency agreement. Anthropic also uses sub-processors for business operations (CRM, ticketing, HR) that do not process API content; the DPA specifies which categories apply.
Sub-processor Change Notification

The DPA specifies how Anthropic notifies customers of sub-processor changes. Typically this is a 30-day advance notice via email or a public sub-processor change log on Anthropic's website. Your procurement process should include subscribing to this notification and documenting your organization's objection procedure (if any).

## Sub-processor Review Checklist

1. [ ] Request current sub-processor list from Anthropic (usually in DPA annex or security portal)
2. [ ] Identify sub-processors that process API content (vs. business ops only)
3. [ ] Confirm each sub-processor's data location (relevant for data residency commitments)
4. [ ] Subscribe to sub-processor change notification (email list or portal)
5. [ ] Document internal objection escalation path if a new sub-processor is added
6. [ ] Confirm sub-processors are covered by the same DPA obligations (DPA chain)
Penetration Testing and Vulnerability Management

Your security team will ask about Anthropic's penetration testing cadence and vulnerability disclosure practices. These controls validate that Anthropic's systems are regularly tested by independent parties and that findings are remediated on a defined schedule.
Control
Anthropic Practice
Evidence to Request
Annual penetration test
Third-party penetration test of the API and supporting infrastructure at least annually
Executive summary (full report is typically not shared externally; confirm with account team)
Bug bounty program
Anthropic operates a bug bounty program via HackerOne for coordinated disclosure of security vulnerabilities
Program listing publicly available; ask for current status and any P0/P1 findings in the past 12 months
Vulnerability remediation SLA
Critical (CVSS ≥9.0) findings remediated within 15 days; high (7.0–8.9) within 30 days
Confirm SLAs in security addendum or questionnaire response
CVE disclosure
Public CVEs affecting API infrastructure disclosed via Anthropic security advisories
Confirm disclosure channel (security.anthropic.com or equivalent)
Model-Specific Security Considerations

Unlike traditional software vendors, Anthropic provides a large language model as the core service. This introduces model-specific security risks that do not appear on a standard security questionnaire but are material for enterprise procurement.
Prompt Injection

Prompt injection is the risk that user-supplied content in a prompt manipulates the model to ignore system instructions or leak information. This is an application-layer risk — Anthropic's infrastructure cannot fully prevent it, but Anthropic's research teams continuously work to make Claude more robust to injection attacks. During due diligence, establish which version of Claude you will use and whether Anthropic provides any contractual representation regarding injection resistance.
Jailbreaking and Policy Bypass

Claude has a trained policy layer (Constitutional AI, safety classifiers) that refuses certain categories of requests. During procurement, confirm: (a) whether Anthropic provides a separate 'safety classifier confidence' or refusal reason in the API response, and (b) how policy violations in your use case are handled — does Anthropic alert you to attempts against your system prompt?
Output Filtering

If your use case requires that model outputs never contain certain content categories (e.g., PII in summarized documents, competitor mentions, medical advice), you are responsible for implementing output filtering at the application layer. Anthropic's built-in safety filters address harms at the model level, not business-specific content policies. Document this responsibility in your risk register.
Incident Response and Breach Notification
Your legal and security teams will require confirmation of Anthropic's incident response and breach notification commitments. Key terms to confirm in the contract:
Breach notification window:

Anthropic's standard DPA specifies notification within 72 hours of Anthropic becoming aware of a personal data breach affecting your data. Confirm this meets your regulatory requirements (GDPR requires 72-hour notification to supervisory authorities; some US state laws require shorter windows).
Scope of notification:
confirm that breach notification covers not just confidentiality breaches (unauthorized disclosure) but also integrity breaches (unauthorized modification of data processed on your behalf).
Incident contact:
establish the channel for receiving security notifications (security advisory email list, account executive, dedicated incident email).
Post-incident report:
request a commitment that Anthropic will provide a post-incident report for P0 incidents affecting your data, including root cause, timeline, and remediation steps.
Completing the Security Questionnaire

Most enterprise procurement processes include a vendor security questionnaire — commonly a Standardised Information Gathering (SIG) Lite, CAIQ, or a custom questionnaire. Anthropic maintains pre-completed questionnaire responses for the most common formats; request the relevant one from your account executive before completing the questionnaire manually.

## Artifacts to Request from Anthropic Account Executive

Security & Compliance:
- [ ] SOC 2 Type II report (current period, issued within 12 months)
- [ ] SOC 2 bridge letter (if report >6 months old)
- [ ] ISO 27001 certificate (if required)
- [ ] Penetration test executive summary (most recent)
- [ ] Pre-filled security questionnaire (SIG Lite, CAIQ, or custom)
- [ ] Security whitepaper / trust center documentation URL

Privacy & Legal:
- [ ] Data Processing Agreement (DPA)
- [ ] Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) — Module 2
- [ ] Sub-processor list (as annex to DPA)
- [ ] Transfer Impact Assessment questionnaire responses
- [ ] BAA (if healthcare / HIPAA use case)

Architecture & Operations:
- [ ] Infrastructure architecture overview (AWS region, availability zones)
- [ ] Data residency confirmation letter (if EU residency required)
- [ ] Incident response and breach notification policy
- [ ] Business continuity / disaster recovery summary
Risk Register Entry Template

After completing due diligence, your security team should document the findings in the corporate vendor risk register. Below is a template for the Anthropic entry, pre-populated with typical findings for a large enterprise Claude API deployment.

## Vendor Risk Register — Anthropic (Claude API)

**Vendor:** Anthropic, PBC
**Service:** Claude API (LLM inference)
**Classification:** Critical (processes business data; core to [use case])
**Review date:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
**Next review:** [YYYY-MM-DD + 12 months]

### Inherent Risks

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Inherent Rating |
|------|-----------|--------|-----------------|
| Cloud provider outage (AWS) affecting API availability | Medium | High | High |
| Data breach at Anthropic (prompt/response logs) | Low | High | Medium |
| Prompt injection enabling data exfiltration | Medium | High | High |
| Model policy change breaking existing use cases | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Regulatory action restricting AI use in jurisdiction | Low | High | Medium |

### Controls and Residual Risk

| Control | Owner | Status |
|---------|-------|--------|
| Zero-retention API mode enabled | Anthropic (contractual) | Confirmed in DPA |
| Application-layer output filtering for PII | [Your team] | Implemented |
| API key scoping (read-only where possible) | [Your team] | Implemented |
| Contract includes breach notification ≤72h | Legal | Confirmed in DPA |
| Annual security review (SOC 2 renewal) | Procurement | Scheduled |

**Residual risk:** Medium — acceptable with above controls
**Risk acceptance:** Approved by CISO [Name] on [Date]
Business Continuity and Availability SLAs

Enterprises integrating Claude into production workflows need to understand Anthropic's availability commitments and what protections exist when those commitments are not met. Unlike traditional SaaS vendors, LLM inference services carry additional availability risk because model inference is computationally intensive and cannot be trivially replicated across providers.
Published SLAs and Uptime Commitments

Anthropic publishes an API status page (status.anthropic.com) with historical uptime data. For enterprise contracts, a separate SLA addendum may specify uptime targets and remedies. Key points to negotiate:
SLA Element
Typical Enterprise Term
Negotiation Leverage
Monthly uptime target
99.9% (8.7h downtime/year) for standard; 99.95% for enterprise tiers
Higher targets available for large annual commitments; confirm scope excludes scheduled maintenance
Downtime credit
Service credits (as % of monthly spend) for misses, not cash refunds
Negotiate minimum credit threshold; credits with no expiry preferable to 30-day windows
Scheduled maintenance window
Typically excluded from SLA calculation; 72-hour advance notice required
Negotiate shorter notice window for critical production use cases
Incident communication
Status page + email for P0/P1 incidents
Request direct phone/Slack notification for enterprise accounts during P0 events
Fallback Architecture Planning

The SOC 2 report and SLA cover Anthropic's obligations, but your architecture must account for API unavailability regardless. During procurement, confirm whether your use case tolerates graceful degradation (queueing requests, returning cached responses) or requires a real-time fallback to another model provider.
Compliance Framework Mapping

Different enterprises operate under different regulatory frameworks. The questions your compliance team will ask depend on which frameworks you are subject to. Below is a mapping of the most common frameworks to the Anthropic-specific evidence you need.
Framework
Key Requirements for Anthropic Use
Evidence Needed
GDPR (EU)
Lawful basis for processing, DPA with processor, SCCs for US transfers, sub-processor chain
DPA + SCCs annex, sub-processor list, transfer impact assessment responses
HIPAA (US Healthcare)
BAA required before sending PHI, zero-retention for PHI, breach notification ≤60 days
BAA from Anthropic, zero-retention confirmation, incident response policy
SOX (Public companies)
Vendor risk management, change management controls for software affecting financial reporting
SOC 2 Type II (change management controls), vendor risk register entry
PCI-DSS (Card data)
Never send cardholder data to AI APIs; confirm scope exclusion
Written confirmation from Anthropic that no PCI-DSS cardholder data is processed; application-layer data masking
FedRAMP (US Federal)
FedRAMP authorization required for federal deployments
Anthropic FedRAMP status: check current authorization level; availability evolves — confirm with account team
ISO 27001 (International)
Supplier information security requirements
Anthropic ISO 27001 certificate + scope of certification

For PCI-DSS, the correct control is never sending cardholder data to the API — not obtaining a PCI certification from Anthropic. Your application layer must mask or exclude card numbers before prompt construction. This is a design control, not a vendor contract control.
Due Diligence Timeline and Process

Enterprise security reviews take time. Planning the due diligence timeline in parallel with commercial negotiations prevents the security review from becoming a blocker after business terms are agreed. A typical enterprise Anthropic procurement runs 4–8 weeks from initial procurement request to signed contract, depending on organization size and internal review complexity.

## Anthropic Procurement Due Diligence Timeline

Week 1: Commercial and Scoping
- [ ] Procurement team receives use case description and data classification
- [ ] Account executive engaged; NDA executed
- [ ] Request artifact package (SOC 2, DPA, SIG Lite, sub-processor list)

Week 2: Security Review
- [ ] InfoSec team reviews SOC 2 report (focus: exceptions, CUECs, observation period)
- [ ] Pen test summary reviewed; open findings documented
- [ ] Zero-retention API mode confirmed if handling regulated data

Week 3: Privacy and Legal Review
- [ ] DPO reviews DPA; negotiates any non-standard terms
- [ ] SCCs confirmed or data residency alternative documented
- [ ] BAA requested if HIPAA in scope

Week 4: Risk Register and Approval
- [ ] Vendor risk register entry drafted
- [ ] Residual risks assessed; controls confirmed
- [ ] CISO sign-off obtained

Week 5-6: Contract Execution
- [ ] Master Service Agreement (MSA) + DPA + SLA addendum finalized
- [ ] Zero-retention and data residency terms in contract, not side letters
- [ ] Procurement records archived in vendor management system

Ongoing (post-contract):
- [ ] Annual SOC 2 renewal review (diarize for contract anniversary)
- [ ] Sub-processor change notification subscription active
- [ ] Incident notification contact maintained and tested annually
Summary

Anthropic security and compliance due diligence covers eight domains: certifications (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001), data handling and retention (zero-retention API, training opt-out), privacy compliance (DPA, SCCs, data subject rights), subprocessors (AWS and change notification), penetration testing and vulnerability management, model-specific risks (prompt injection, jailbreaking, output filtering), incident response and breach notification, and security questionnaire completion. For each domain, request specific artifacts from your account executive before contract execution. Document residual risks in your vendor risk register with explicit control mapping and sign-off from your CISO or equivalent.
